title: Approximate Irregular Shape with Rectangle
description: Estimate the area of an irregular region by modeling it as a rectangle.
condition: An irregular region is given and dimensions can be reasonably approximated by length and width.
response: Treat the region as a rectangle and identify approximate length and width.
label: Rule / Plan
example: Model the park as a rectangle about 50 mi wide and 60 mi tall.

title: Area Scales by Square of Scale Factor
description: Understand that when lengths are scaled by a factor $k$, areas are scaled by $k^2$.
condition: Given a scale factor $k$ between similar figures.
response: Multiply area by $k^2$ to find the new area.
label: Principle
example: If a figure is scaled by $\tfrac{1}{2}$, its area is scaled by $\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \tfrac{1}{4}$.

title: Check Corresponding Angles for Congruence
description: Determine whether all corresponding angles in two figures are equal when assessing if they are scaled copies.
condition: Given two figures with labeled corresponding angle measures.
response: Verify that each pair of corresponding angles has equal measure.
label: Concept
example: If one figure has angles $90^\circ, 135^\circ, 45^\circ$ and the other has the same three measures in corresponding positions, then the angles are congruent.
notes: Equality of all corresponding angles is required for scaled copies.

title: Check Proportional Side Lengths
description: Determine whether corresponding side lengths of two figures are related by a single common scale factor.
condition: Given two figures with corresponding side lengths identified.
response: Compute ratios of corresponding sides and verify they are all equal.
label: Principle / Model
example: If corresponding sides are $4$ and $8$, $5$ and $10$, $7$ and $14$, then the ratios are $\frac{8}{4}=2$, $\frac{10}{5}=2$, $\frac{14}{7}=2$, so the scale factor is consistent.

title: Conclude Figures Are Scaled Copies
description: Determine that two figures are scaled copies when corresponding angles are equal and corresponding sides are proportional.
condition: Given two figures where all corresponding angles are congruent and all corresponding side lengths share a common ratio.
response: State that the figures are scaled copies (similar).
label: Principle / Model
example: If all corresponding angles match and each side length in Figure B is $2$ times the corresponding side in Figure A, then Figure B is a scaled copy of Figure A.

title: Convert Units Within a Measurement System
description: Convert measurements within the same system using known conversion factors (e.g., meters to centimeters).
condition: Given a measurement and a required unit within the same system.
response: Multiply or divide by the appropriate conversion factor (e.g., $1\text{ m} = 100\text{ cm}$).
label: Principle

title: Identify Corresponding Parts in Similar Figures
description: Match corresponding sides or angles between a figure and its scaled copy based on vertex order or structure.
condition: Given two similar figures with labeled vertices.
response: Determine which sides or angles correspond based on consistent vertex order.
label: Principle
example: If quadrilateral $ABCD$ is a scaled copy of $EFGH$, then side $AB$ corresponds to $EF$ and angle $ADC$ corresponds to angle $EHG$.

title: Identify Non-Scaled Copy
description: Determine that two figures are not scaled copies if either corresponding angles differ or side ratios are inconsistent.
condition: Given two figures where at least one pair of corresponding angles is unequal or side length ratios are not all equal.
response: State that the figures are not scaled copies.
label: Principle / Model
example: If corresponding sides have ratios $\frac{6}{9}=\frac{2}{3}$ and $\frac{9}{12}=\frac{3}{4}$, the ratios differ, so the figures are not scaled copies.

title: Interpret Stated Scale Ratio
description: Understand a written scale such as 1 inch to 4 miles as a multiplicative relationship.
condition: A scale is stated in the form 1 unit on map equals k units in reality (e.g., 1 in : 4 mi).
response: Recognize that each 1 unit on the map corresponds to k actual units and set up proportional reasoning.
label: Concept
example: A scale of 1 in : 4 mi means every 1 inch on the map represents 4 miles in reality.

title: Recognize Angles Are Preserved Under Scaling
description: Understand that scaling a figure changes side lengths proportionally but preserves angle measures.
condition: Given a figure and a scale factor applied to create a scaled copy.
response: Keep all corresponding angle measures the same in the scaled figure.
label: Principle / Model
example: If the original triangle has angles $30^\circ$, $60^\circ$, and $90^\circ$, the scaled copy also has angles $30^\circ$, $60^\circ$, and $90^\circ$.

title: Scale Length by Scale Factor
description: Multiply or divide a length by a scale factor to move between original and scaled figures.
condition: Given a length and a scale factor between figures.
response: Multiply (to enlarge) or divide (to reduce) by the scale factor.
label: Principle
example: At a scale of $1:40$, an actual length of $6\text{ m}$ becomes $6 \div 40 = 0.15\text{ m}$ on the drawing.

title: Set Up Proportion from Scale Statement
description: Translate a scale statement into a proportion relating map and actual distances.
condition: Given a scale such as 3 cm represents 5 km.
response: Write and solve a proportion relating map distance to actual distance.
label: Principle
example: If $3\text{ cm} : 5\text{ km}$, then $\frac{3}{5} = \frac{x}{15}$, so $x = 9\text{ cm}$.

title: Use Scale Bar to Determine Actual Distance
description: Given a measured map length and a scale bar, determine the corresponding actual distance.
condition: A map length is measured and a scale bar shows a known map-to-actual distance relationship.
response: Use proportional reasoning to convert the measured map length to actual distance.
label: Principle / Model
example: If 1 cm on the map represents 20 miles and the south border measures 2.5 cm, then actual length is $2.5 \times 20 = 50$ miles.

title: Verify Equivalent Ratios
description: Determine whether two ratios are equivalent by simplifying or comparing multiplicative factors.
condition: Given two pairs of numbers claimed to form equal ratios.
response: Simplify or scale both ratios to check for equivalence.
label: Principle
example: Check if $40:10$ matches $8:2$. Since $40 \div 8 = 5$ and $10 \div 2 = 5$, the ratios are equivalent.
